Technical reference: child algorithm

2026-07-20

Source: vignettes/child-algorithm.Rmd
child-algorithm.Rmd

Technical reference for the child algorithm (cleanchild() in child_clean.R and supporting functions).

This document describes what the current R code does in the child algorithm. It is intended as a debugging aid and a long-term technical reference. References to code use function names and step numbers rather than line numbers, because line numbers become stale with each code edit.

How to Read This Document

This reference documents the child algorithm cleaning steps (Child Steps 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15/16, 17, 19, 21, 22) plus child-specific concepts, the cleanchild() working dataframe, and child-only output material.

Wrapper-level material lives in vignette("wrapper"): input format, unit conversion, the full z-score calculation pipeline (CSD method, WHO/CDC blending, recentering), batching, dispatch, output reassembly, the Child Step 2b GA correction (which runs in cleangrowth() preprocessing), and the canonical Code review checklist applied to each step.

Each algorithm step section here has:

  • A summary table (scope, prior/next step, exclusion codes, code location)
  • Overview
  • Key terms and variable names
  • Logic and implementation
  • Rationale for selected decisions
  • Configurable parameters in scope
  • Variables created and dropped
  • Checklist findings

Sections that do not apply to a given step are marked “N/A” rather than omitted.

This document covers, in order:

  • Child-specific Key Concepts
  • Z-Score Summary (brief; full mechanics in wrapper reference)
  • cleanchild() main algorithm structure
  • Working Dataframe and Output (child-specific mechanics)
  • Variable Glossary (child-specific variables)
  • Output Format (child exclusion codes + CF rescue codes)
  • Configurable Parameters (child-specific)
  • Complete List of Steps
  • Step-by-step walkthroughs (Early Child Step 13 through Child Step 22)

Key Concepts

This section covers concepts specific to the child algorithm. For wrapper-level concepts (data retention, CSD z-scores, prematurity correction, recentering, three growth parameters, internal_id tiebreaking), see vignette("wrapper") → Key Concepts (wrapper-level).

Three growth parameters (child scope): The child algorithm cleans WEIGHTKG, HEIGHTCM (including LENGTHCM relabeled to HEIGHTCM; see length.adjust parameter), and HEADCM. HEADCM is cleaned through age 5 years — HC measurements with agedays > 5 × 365.25 are marked Exclude-Not-Cleaned (reported as Exclude-HC-Out-of-Range) because the WHO HC z-score reference ends at 5 years, so recentered z-scores cannot be formed beyond that. The recentering file’s HC rcmedian.sd is carried forward from its 2.75-year (1004-day) value for all ages > 2.75y out to 5 years: HC is rarely measured after ~2.75y, so recentering on the sparse later values is avoided and the 2.75y value stands in for older children. For HC ages 24 months–5 years, the velocity check (Child Step 17) uses only mindiff = -1.5 with no upper bound since the WHO HC velocity reference ends at 24 months; EWMA steps provide the primary outlier detection for this range.

Designated Other Parameter (DOP): Many steps compare a measurement against a different growth parameter for the same subject. The designated other parameter (DOP) for weight is height; the DOP for height is weight; the DOP for HC is height. DOP comparisons are used in temporary SDE resolution (Child Step 5), CF rescue, EWMA steps, final SDE resolution (Child Step 13), and pairs/singles evaluation (Child Step 19). In Child Step 13, DOP medians are computed for one-day SDE resolution (cross-parameter comparison) and DOP is used as a secondary sort key for selecting which SDE to keep.

Ordered steps: Like the adult algorithm, the child algorithm processes steps in a specific order, removing more extreme problems first and getting more refined later. Same-day extraneous values (described below) are handled in repeated steps because of bidirectional impact of same-day selection and other exclusions. Some errors cause over- or under-detection of other errors, so they must be excluded first. The order of steps is central to the algorithm.

Iterations within steps: Several steps (Evil Twins, EWMA1, EWMA2) are iterative. If more than one value is a candidate for exclusion, they select the most extreme candidate and then re-evaluate for further exclusions. This continues until no candidates remain or too few values are left to evaluate. This is a central part of the algorithm and reduces the likelihood of erroneous values leading to exclusion of true values.

Temporary vs. permanent exclusions: Same-day extraneous (SDE) values are multiple values for the same parameter on the same day. growthcleanr selects the most plausible SDE for inclusion in the final dataset. SDEs are first flagged temporarily (Child Step 5) and later resolved permanently (Child Step 13). Carried-forward values may be “rescued” and returned to Include status based on z-score similarity criteria. These temporary mechanisms allow the algorithm to refine decisions as the dataset gets cleaner.

No separate parameter pipelines: Unlike the adult algorithm, which processes height and weight in completely separate working dataframes, the child algorithm keeps all parameters in a single data.table (data.df) with parameter tracked in a param column. Child Steps that operate on a single parameter use by = .(subjid, param) groupings or explicit filtering. This design supports the cross-parameter comparisons (DOP) that are central to the child algorithm.

CF rescue: The child algorithm has a carried-forward (CF) rescue system (Child Step 6) that attempts to distinguish genuinely repeated measurements from data-entry artifacts. In the default cf_rescue = "standard" mode, each string of identical values is judged on how far the carried value has drifted from the real value it copies and how the trajectory continues past it; cf_rescue = "none" and cf_rescue = "all" provide all-or-nothing behavior. Rescued CFs are returned to Include. See vignette("cf-rescue") for the full rule.

EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average): The central mechanism for detecting implausible values. For each measurement, the EWMA computes a weighted average of the subject’s other measurements for the same parameter, giving exponentially more weight to temporally closer values. The deviation from EWMA (dewma) indicates how far a value is from what we would predict. Default exponent is -1.5 (child) vs. -5 (adult). With -1.5, the weighting is less dominated by the nearest neighbor, spreading influence across a wider time range — appropriate for pediatric data where growth trajectories change more rapidly.

The .child_valid() function: A critical gatekeeper that determines which rows participate in each step. See Support Functions → .child_valid() below for flag semantics and behavior.

Age-dependent internal_id tiebreaking: When resolving same-day duplicates, the child algorithm uses an age-dependent rule based on internal_id (the sequential integer assigned in id-sorted order at session start): at birth (agedays == 0), keep the lowest internal_id (earliest measurement, before postnatal fluid shifts and interventions); at all other ages, keep the highest internal_id (later measurement, which may represent a more careful re-measurement). This differs from the adult algorithm, which always keeps the highest internal_id. The user’s original id column is never used as a tiebreaker; it is preserved untouched into output.

Support Functions

Child-algorithm-internal helpers defined in R/child_clean.R. Each entry lists purpose, callers, input/return contract, key invariants the function assumes at entry, and the enclosing function name as a code anchor. For wrapper-level helpers that both algorithms share, see vignette("wrapper") → Shared Helpers.

.child_valid(df, include.temporary.extraneous = FALSE, include.extraneous = FALSE, include.carryforward = FALSE)

Purpose. The central row-eligibility filter for every step. Returns a logical vector marking which rows are currently “valid” for the step that called it.

Callers. Called 18 times across cleanchild(), at the entry of every step that needs to filter rows.

Inputs and return contract.

  • df — either a data.frame / data.table containing an exclude column, or a plain character / factor vector of exclusion codes. Coerced to character internally, so factor and character inputs behave identically.
  • include.* flags — three additive booleans that opt specific soft-excluded categories back in: include.temporary.extraneous (Child Step 5 temp SDEs), include.extraneous (Child Step 13 permanent SDEs), include.carryforward (Child Step 6 CFs).
  • Returns a logical vector the same length as the input. TRUE = keep for processing.

Default behavior. Base set is !grepl("^Exclude", exclude). Because every exclusion code in the child algorithm (Exclude-Missing, Exclude-Not-Cleaned, Exclude-C-*) starts with "Exclude-", the default call filters out all excluded rows and admits only "Include".

Key invariants. The exclude column is populated for every row by the time any step calls .child_valid() — Exclude-Missing and Exclude-Not-Cleaned are set pre-dispatch by cleangrowth(), and step-level exclusion codes are written in place. A row cannot have NA in exclude.

Code location. R/child_clean.R, function .child_valid(). No reverse dependencies on identify_temp_sde() or the other support functions — .child_valid() uses only the exclude column.

.child_exc(param_val, suffix)

Purpose. Generates a child exclusion code from a reason suffix. Returns paste0("Exclude-C-", suffix).

Callers. ~50 call sites across cleanchild() — essentially every place that writes an Exclude-C-* code.

Inputs and return contract.

  • param_val — accepted but ignored. Retained only to avoid churning the ~50 call sites that pass it. Can safely be any value, including an unused column reference inside a data.table j expression.
  • suffix — reason code (e.g. "PIV", "CF", "Traj", "Evil-Twins", "Temp-Same-Day", "Unevaluable-Trajectory"). Must correspond to a level in exclude.levels or the factor assignment silently produces NA.
  • Returns a character scalar or vector (vectorised via paste0).

Key invariants. The full canonical list of allowed suffix values is in vignette("exclusion-codes"). Adding a new suffix requires also adding the corresponding code to exclude.levels in cleangrowth().

Code location. R/child_clean.R, function .child_exc().

get_dop(param_name)

Purpose. Scalar lookup of the designated other parameter (DOP) for a given growth parameter.

Callers. Two sites inside cleanchild() — in the Child Step 15/16 block (the last-ext DOP lookup) and in the Child Step 19 pairs/singles block. Both callers pass df$param[1] from a single-param working subset.

Inputs and return contract.

  • param_name — scalar character: "WEIGHTKG", "HEIGHTCM", or "HEADCM".
  • Returns a scalar character: the DOP. WEIGHTKG -> HEIGHTCM, HEIGHTCM -> WEIGHTKG, HEADCM -> HEIGHTCM (no reverse mapping; HC’s DOP is height, not the other way around).

Key invariants. Scalar-only — the internal if / else if / else chain requires a scalar logical test. Vector input produces an R warning / error. No validation: anything other than "WEIGHTKG" or "HEIGHTCM" falls through to the HEADCM branch.

Code location. R/child_clean.R, function get_dop().

identify_temp_sde(df, exclude_from_dop_ids = NULL)

Purpose. Selects the “keeper” on each (subjid, param, agedays) group that has more than one valid measurement, marking the rest for a temporary same-day-extraneous flag. The keeper is the row closest to the subject-parameter tbc.sd median (primary) and the cross-parameter DOP tbc.sd median (secondary), with an age-dependent internal_id tiebreaker.

Callers. Seven sites inside cleanchild(). Only the Step 13 caller passes a non-NULL exclude_from_dop_ids:

Step exclude_from_dop_ids Purpose
Step 5 (initial) NULL First temp-SDE pass
Step 6 (recalc) NULL After CF identification
Step 7d (recalc) NULL After PIV
Step 9d (recalc) NULL After Evil Twins
Step 11 mid-loop (subset recalc) NULL After each EWMA1 iteration
Step 11c end-of-step NULL Full-dataset refresh
Step 13 temp_sde_ids_step13 Final SDE, with DOP anchor restricted

Inputs and return contract.

  • df — data.table column-subset containing id, internal_id, subjid, param, agedays, tbc.sd, exclude, and orig_row. Not mutated.
  • exclude_from_dop_ids — optional vector of id values to remove from the DOP median calculation only (they still contribute to the SP median).
  • Returns a logical vector aligned to the caller’s row order. TRUE marks rows the caller should flag "Exclude-C-Temp-Same-Day"; FALSE means leave exclude unchanged.

Key invariant. Identical same-day values are removed earlier (Early Child Step 13 SDE-Identicals), so by the time identify_temp_sde() runs, all same-day values in any given (subjid, param, agedays) group are dissimilar.

Robust SP median. A single clearly-extreme value that has not yet been excluded by a later trajectory step can drag the (subjid, param) tbc.sd median toward it and make the wrong same-day value win. Before computing the SP median, the function compares each Include value to the group’s provisional median; if exactly one Include value is > 3 z from it, that value is dropped from the median computation (both the primary median.spz and, in the Step 13 path, the DOP-restricted median). The dropped value still receives a median and is still SDE-evaluated — it is only barred from anchoring the median. far is evaluated for Include rows only, and the “exactly one” guard means two mutually-distant values (which cannot be disambiguated) are both kept.

Code location. R/child_clean.R, function identify_temp_sde().

calc_otl_evil_twins(df)

Purpose. Marks each row with otl = TRUE if it is the inner member (B or C) of an anchored over-the-limit pair. “OTL” stands for “over the limit.” See the Evil Twins (Child Step 9) → OTL calculation section below for the full anchored-pair walkthrough; this entry is the support-function summary.

Callers. One site: Child Step 9 (Evil Twins) inside cleanchild().

Inputs and return contract.

  • df — data.table with subjid, param, tbc.sd, ctbc.sd at minimum, sorted by (subjid, param, agedays, internal_id).
  • Returns df with an added otl boolean column. On nrow(df) < 4L inputs (too few rows to form an A,B,C,D group) it sets otl = FALSE for all rows and returns early.

Logic. Within four consecutive eligible rows A, B, C, D (in sort order), the inner pair (B, C) is over the limit when its jump exceeds 5 on both tbc.sd and ctbc.sd; it is anchored when A holds B to within 3 on either scale and D holds C to within 3 on either scale. When both hold, B and C are flagged OTL; the outer anchors A and D are not. A missing flank (no A before B, or no D after C) disqualifies the pair, so single end-spikes and singles embedded between normals are ceded to the EWMA steps. Implemented with shift(..., by = .(subjid, param)) (one- and two-away lag/lead of tbc.sd/ctbc.sd); the per-SP shift() returns NA at subject-parameter boundaries, so comparisons never cross SPs.

Key invariants. Adjacent-pair comparison assumes the caller’s sort is (subjid, param, agedays, internal_id) — neighbors in row order are neighbors in age within the same subject-parameter. The jump threshold is strict > 5 on both tbc.sd and ctbc.sd; the anchor threshold is <= 3 on either scale (lenient). Requiring both scales to jump prevents a false positive when GA correction moves only one of the two z-scores for a potcorr subject.

Code location. R/child_clean.R, function calc_otl_evil_twins().

ewma(agedays, z, ewma.exp, ewma.adjacent = TRUE, window = 15, cache_env = NULL)

Purpose. Core exponentially-weighted moving-average helper. For each observation i, computes a weighted average of all other observations in the group, with weights (5 + |agedays_i - agedays_j|) ^ ewma.exp_i and w_ii = 0. Returns three variants (ewma.all, ewma.before, ewma.after) that exclude different subsets of the group. It operates on a single z-score vector per call; the corrected score is obtained by a second ewma() call on ctbc.sd (the two calls share a delta matrix via cache_env).

Callers. Three direct call sites in cleanchild():

  • Child Step 11 (EWMA1 Extreme) — iterative; calls ewma() directly inside a per-group closure, once on tbc.sd and once on ctbc.sd, passing a shared cache_env so the two calls reuse one age-gap delta matrix. (This is not the incremental ewma_cache_* API.)
  • Child Step 13 — final SDE resolution (ewma_df).
  • Child Step 17 — height/HC velocity tiebreaker for 3+-measurement groups (recomputed from scratch each iteration, not cached).

Child Step 15/16 (EWMA2 Moderate) does not call ewma() directly — it uses the incremental ewma_cache_init() / ewma_cache_update() machinery instead (see next entry).

Inputs and return contract.

  • agedays — integer vector.
  • z — numeric z-score vector, aligned with agedays (callers pass tbc.sd on the first call and ctbc.sd on the second).
  • ewma.exp — scalar or per-observation vector. All in-algorithm callers pass a per-observation vector that varies by widest neighbor age gap.
  • ewma.adjacent — if FALSE, only ewma.all is returned (one-element list).
  • window — max Include observations on each side to include; Inf disables windowing.
  • cache_env — optional environment; when supplied, the windowed age-gap delta matrix is computed once and reused across paired calls on the same agedays + exponents (e.g. the tbc.sd then ctbc.sd calls in Step 11).
  • Returns a named list of numeric vectors (not a data.frame). Single-element when ewma.adjacent = FALSE.

Key invariants. Self-weight is zero, so observation i is excluded from its own EWMA. Groups with n <= 2 follow first/last-row conventions documented inline. The +5 constant prevents infinite weight for zero-gap pairs; the negative ewma.exp makes weight decay with gap.

Code location. R/child_clean.R, function ewma().

ewma_cache_init(...) / ewma_cache_update(...)

Purpose. Incremental EWMA machinery for the iterative EWMA2 loops (Child Steps 15/16). ewma_cache_init() builds a per-group weight matrix and initial cached weighted sums; ewma_cache_update() removes an excluded observation from the cache in O(n) arithmetic without rebuilding the matrix. (EWMA1 — Child Step 11 — does not use this API; it calls ewma() directly with a shared cache_env delta matrix, see previous entry.)

Callers. Used by the EWMA2 (Child Step 15 and Child Step 16) iteration loops in cleanchild(); not called by any other step.

Inputs and return contract.

  • ewma_cache_init() — takes the same arguments as ewma() plus initial exclude state. Returns an environment with the weight matrix, cached row sums, and cached weighted sums per variant.
  • ewma_cache_update() — takes the environment plus the position of the newly-excluded observation; mutates the environment in place to reflect the removal, checking up to 2 positions on each side for max-gap shifts.
  • The child ewma() function reads from the environment when supplied with cache_env.

Code location. R/child_clean.R, functions ewma_cache_init() and ewma_cache_update().

as_matrix_delta(agedays)

Purpose. Internal helper that builds an n × n matrix of pairwise absolute age differences from an agedays vector. Used by ewma() and ewma_cache_init() for building the age-gap weight matrix.

Callers. Two sites, both in the EWMA machinery above.

Inputs and return contract. Takes a numeric vector; returns a square numeric matrix. No edge-case guards — a zero-length input returns a 0 × 0 matrix.

Code location. R/child_clean.R, function as_matrix_delta().

calc_and_recenter_z_scores(df, cn, ref.data.path, measurement.to.z = NULL, measurement.to.z_who = NULL)

Purpose. Recalculates recentered z-scores for a perturbed measurement column (p_plus / p_minus). Applies the same WHO/CDC blending window and NA-fallback logic as the main cleangrowth() z-score calculation, then subtracts the precomputed rcmedian.sd. The perturbed z-scores (tbc.p_plus, tbc.p_minus) are computed once for all rows in cleangrowth() preprocessing (the recentering block) and ride in on data.df, so the trajectory steps that consume them (the Child Step 11 and Child Step 15/16 plus/minus checks) read them directly without rebuilding per batch.

Callers. Two sites in cleangrowth() preprocessing — in the recentering block, right after tbc.sd/ctbc.sd are formed — once with cn = "p_plus" and once with cn = "p_minus". Both callers pass pre-built measurement.to.z / measurement.to.z_who closures to avoid repeat disk reads. (The NULL-closure fallback in the function body exists only for direct / debugging use.)

Inputs and return contract.

  • df — data.table with param, agedays, sex, rcmedian.sd, and the measurement column named by cn. Modified in place: intermediate cn.orig_cdc, cn.orig_who, cn.orig, and a new tbc.<cn> column are added.
  • cn — character scalar column name.
  • ref.data.path — only consulted when the closures are NULL.
  • Returns df with the new tbc.<cn> column. Intermediate cn.orig* columns are left on df but are not consumed downstream.

Key invariants. rcmedian.sd must already be populated by the main recentering step; the helper does not re-merge the recentering file.

Code location. R/child_clean.R, function calc_and_recenter_z_scores(). For the underlying z-score mechanics (CSD formula, blending boundaries, NA-fallback logic), see vignette("wrapper") → Z-Score Infrastructure.

.compute_corrected_z(dt, meas, measurement.to.z, measurement.to.z_who)

Purpose. The Child Step 2b gestational-age corrected-z engine. Computes the corrected, not-yet-recentered CSD z-score for an arbitrary measurement vector, holding the GA-correction parameters (cagedays, Fenton M/S_upper/S_lower) fixed at the values derived from the subject’s actual first weight, so the same correction is applied to the real measurement and to the ±5%-wt / ±1 cm plus/minus perturbations. Three components combined by age: (1) the uncorrected WHO/CDC blend at chronological age (mirrors the main z blend), (2) the Fenton CSD z ((v_fenton − M)/(S_side · M), with WT scaled ×1000 to grams) at gestational-corrected age, and (3) the corrected WHO/CDC z at cagedays. The +0.8/+0.7 length-adjust is added to HT for agedays > 730 & cagedays <= 730. HC uses WHO at all ages; HT/WT blend WHO→CDC over ages 2–5. Fenton is preferred over corrected-WHO for ages ≤2 (when available). The final corrected z: ≤2y uses sd.c; 2–4y blends sd.c with the uncorrected sd.orig_m; else uncorrected. Returns the corrected z vector; subtract rcmedian.sd downstream to get ctbc.

Inputs. dt must carry param, agedays, sex, ageyears_2b, cagedays, M, S_upper, S_lower, potcorr; meas is the measurement vector to z-score (same length/order as dt); the two closures are the CDC-only and WHO-blended read_anthro() closures.

Callers. Three sites in cleangrowth() Step 2b preprocessing — once for the actual value v, once for p_plus, once for p_minus.

Code location. R/child_clean.R, function .compute_corrected_z(). For the GA-correction narrative (potcorr selection, the corroboration gate), see “Gestational Age Correction (Child Step 2b)” below and vignette("wrapper").

.child_catchup_protect(agedays, tbc.sd, ctbc.sd)

Purpose. Given one (subjid, param) group’s vectors sorted by agedays, returns a logical vector marking values to PROTECT from trajectory exclusion because they are members of an early preterm catch-up increase. A consecutive Include pair (earlier → later) qualifies when the earlier member is younger than 3 months, the z rises from the earlier to the later member, and the rise stays within allowed = min(4, 2 + 0.5 × ceiling(max(0, interval_months − 1))) on at least one of the tbc / ctbc frames (2 z for the first month, +0.5 z per full-or-partial month after, capped at 4). Both members of a qualifying pair are marked; a value is then kept only if its own tbc.sd <= -1.5 (genuinely low), since above that gate the corrected frame is ignored.

Callers. Child Steps 11, 15, and 16, applied only to GA-corrected subjects (gated on ctbc.sd != tbc.sd somewhere in the group), after candidate marking and before worst-value selection. There is no separate corroboration gate here: GA correction itself is now corroboration-gated (see Child Step 2b), so only genuinely-corroborated preterms reach this protection. See “Preterm catch-up protection” under EWMA2 (Child Steps 15/16).

Contract. Pure function of the three input vectors; returns a logical vector aligned to the input rows. It can only remove values from candidacy, never add an exclusion.

Cross-references to wrapper helpers

The child algorithm also uses several wrapper-level helpers; their detailed treatment lives in vignette("wrapper") → Shared Helpers:

  • read_anthro(path, cdc.only) — builds CDC / WHO closures from inst/extdata/ reference tables. Called once per closure at the top of cleangrowth() (and as a fallback inside calc_and_recenter_z_scores() when closures are not supplied).
  • gc_preload_refs(path) — pre-loads both closures as a list for repeated cleangrowth() calls (~0.9 s saved per call).
  • sd_median(param, sex, agedays, sd.orig) — an earlier helper for deriving recentering medians by day of age. Not called at runtime, and not how the current file was built: the shipped rcfile-resmoothed.csv.gz comes from a separate smoothing procedure (see vignette("recentering")).

Architecture: cleanchild()

The child algorithm cleaning loop runs in cleanchild() in child_clean.R. All preprocessing (input validation, unit conversion, z-score calculation, recentering, GA correction, batching, dispatch) is performed by cleangrowth() before cleanchild() is called — see vignette("wrapper") → Architecture: cleangrowth().

cleanchild() — main algorithm (child_clean.R)

Processes one batch of pediatric data through all cleaning steps:

  • Early Child Step 13: SDE-Identicals
  • Child Step 5: Temporary SDEs
  • Child Step 6: Carried Forwards (with rescue)
  • Child Step 7: PIV (Absolute and Standardized)
  • Child Step 9: Evil Twins
  • Child Step 11: Extreme EWMA (EWMA1)
  • Child Step 13: Final SDE Resolution
  • Child Step 15: Moderate EWMA (EWMA2)
  • Child Step 16: Birth HT/HC (EWMA2 variant for birth values)
  • Child Step 17: Height/HC Velocity Checks
  • Child Step 19: Pairs and Singles Evaluation
  • Child Step 21: Error Load
  • Child Step 22: Output Preparation

Support functions are defined in child_clean.R (all child helpers live in the same file as cleanchild(); utils.R is reserved for cross-algorithm utilities). Full per-function documentation — purpose, callers, inputs, invariants, and code locations — is in the Support Functions section above. Quick index:

  • .child_valid() — row-eligibility filter (called 18× across steps)
  • .child_exc() — generate Exclude-C-* codes (~50 call sites)
  • get_dop() — designated other parameter lookup
  • identify_temp_sde() — temp SDE resolution (7 call sites)
  • calc_otl_evil_twins() — Evil Twins OTL calculation
  • ewma() + ewma_cache_init() / ewma_cache_update() + as_matrix_delta() — EWMA machinery
  • calc_and_recenter_z_scores() — p_plus / p_minus perturbation z-scores, computed in cleangrowth() preprocessing, consumed by the Child Step 11 and 15/16 plus/minus checks
  • .compute_corrected_z() — Child Step 2b GA corrected-z engine (actual value + perturbations)

Wrapper-level helpers (read_anthro(), gc_preload_refs(), sd_median()) are documented in vignette("wrapper") → Shared Helpers.

The unit-error-range helper family (.child_z3(), .compute_unit_error_range(), and the shared .unit_error_range_flag() band logic) — which builds the default-ON informational unit_error_range output column and feeds the Child Step 9 Evil Twins uer input — is documented in the cross-algorithm vignette("methods").

Batching, Dispatch, and Data Requirements

These topics are wrapper-level. See:

  • vignette("wrapper") → Batching and Dispatch (subject-level batching, outer/inner two-system structure, parallel processing, partial runs via cached_results / changed_subjids, preloaded references via gc_preload_refs())
  • vignette("wrapper") → Data Requirements and Input Format (input vectors, unit conversion, sorting, internal identifiers)

High-level summary of the z-score variants the child algorithm uses. The full mechanics — CSD formula, reference tables, WHO/CDC blending formulas, recentering procedure, and the calc_and_recenter_z_scores() helper — live in vignette("wrapper") → Z-Score Infrastructure.

Main z-score pipeline (tbc.sd)

  1. CSD calculation. For each measurement, compute a Conditional Standard Deviation z-score against WHO and CDC reference tables: (measurement − M) / csd_pos if measurement >= M, else (measurement − M) / csd_neg. Produces sd.orig_who and sd.orig_cdc.
  2. WHO/CDC age blending. Smooth transition from WHO (< 2 years) to CDC (> 5 years) with a linear blend over 2–5 years. HEADCM uses WHO at all ages. Produces sd.orig.
  3. Recentering. Subtract a fixed age/sex/param-specific population median to produce tbc.sd = sd.orig − rcmedian.sd. Recentering removes age-related drift in median z-scores so that comparisons across age are unbiased.

tbc.sd is the primary score used by most algorithm steps (CF rescue, PIV, Evil Twins, EWMA1/2, velocity).

Corrected z-score pipeline (ctbc.sd, potcorr subjects only)

For very preterm subjects (those whose first weight is consistent with prematurity) a corrected z-score path runs in parallel:

  1. potcorr identification → AGA conversion. The Child Step 2b GA correction (which runs in cleangrowth() preprocessing — full details in vignette("wrapper") → Gestational Age Correction) flags potcorr subjects, derives Fenton GA from first weight, and produces sd.corr using Fenton 2025 CSD z-scores at corrected age (with corrected-WHO/CDC as fallback above 2 years and a smoothed corrected→original blend over 2–4 years).
  2. Recentering. Subtract the same recentering medians: ctbc.sd = sd.corr − rcmedian.sd.

ctbc.sd is used alongside tbc.sd in Evil Twins and EWMA steps to prevent excluding values that are plausible for a baby with very low birth weight.

Variants reference

Variable Description
sd.orig_who, sd.orig_cdc Per-reference CSD z-scores
sd.orig WHO/CDC age-blended CSD z-score
sd.orig_uncorr Copy of sd.orig before GA correction; used in CF rescue (Child Step 6) and PIV (Child Step 7)
sd.corr Fenton-corrected z-score for potcorr; equals sd.orig for others
tbc.sd sd.orig − rcmedian.sd (primary score)
ctbc.sd sd.corr − rcmedian.sd (used alongside tbc.sd)

Working Dataframe and Output

How exclusions work

The algorithm never removes rows from the output. Every input row receives an exclude code — either "Include" or an exclusion code indicating why and at which step the value was excluded.

Internally, most steps operate on the full data.df data.table but filter to currently valid rows using the .child_valid() function. When the reference says a value is “excluded,” this means:

  1. The value’s exclude column is set to the appropriate exclusion code
  2. Subsequent calls to .child_valid() will exclude this row from processing
  3. The value remains in data.df with its exclusion code

There is also a softer mechanism: the temporary SDE flag. Values marked "Exclude-C-Temp-Same-Day" are excluded by default .child_valid() calls but can be included by passing include.temporary.extraneous = TRUE. This allows steps to optionally include or exclude temp SDEs. Permanent SDE resolution (Child Step 13) replaces temp SDE codes with final codes.

Key difference from adult algorithm

The adult algorithm copies rows into separate working dataframes (h_subj_df, w_subj_df) that shrink as values are excluded. The child algorithm keeps all rows in a single data.df and uses the exclude column plus .child_valid() to control which rows participate. Rows are never physically removed from data.df during processing (except for a temporary removal of SDE-Identicals during CF string detection, which are re-added after).

Variable Glossary

For input/identification variables (line, id, internal_id, subjid, param, agedays, sex, v, v.orig), z-score variables (sd.orig*, sd.corr, rcmedian.sd, tbc.sd, ctbc.sd), and prematurity correction variables (potcorr, potcorr_wt, uncorr, fengadays, pmagedays, cagedays), see vignette("wrapper") → Variable Glossary (wrapper-level).

This section covers child-specific variables only.

Exclusion and status variables

Variable Type Created Description
exclude factor Preprocessing Exclusion code or “Include”
cf_rescued character Child Step 6 CF rescue reason; empty string for non-rescued rows
nnte logical Preprocessing Always FALSE in current code. Retained as a placeholder and has no effect on results.

Carried-forward variables (Child Step 6)

All of these are internal Child Step 6 working columns; all are dropped before Child Step 6 exits (via cols_to_drop_6 or earlier cleanup).

Variable Type Role
v.orig numeric Copy of the raw measurement (created in Child Step 5, used in Child Step 6 detection).
cf_binary logical TRUE if the row is currently Exclude-C-CF. Temp, cleaned mid-step.
originator logical TRUE for an Include whose next row (subject/param/ageday sort order) is a CF. Temp, cleaned mid-step.
originator_seq integer cumsum(originator) per subject-param. Temp, cleaned mid-step.
cf_string_num / cs integer Sequential string number per subject-param. cs is the alias retained through the rescue phase.
originator_z numeric The originator’s sd.orig_uncorr z-score, propagated forward to its CFs. Temp, cleaned mid-step.
seq_win integer Position within a CF string: 0 for originator, 1, 2, 3, ... for subsequent CFs.
absdiff numeric |sd.orig_uncorr − originator_z| for each CF.
ageday_has_include logical TRUE if any row on the same (subjid, param, agedays) has exclude == "Include". Restricts CF rescue eligibility (not originator assignment).
orig_ageday integer The originator’s agedays, propagated forward to its CFs. Used to compute the cf_dage_orig design-aid column under cf_detail.

EWMA variables (internal, not in output)

Variable Type Description
ewma.all numeric EWMA excluding only the current observation
ewma.before numeric EWMA excluding current + prior observation
ewma.after numeric EWMA excluding current + next observation
dewma.all numeric tbc.sd - ewma.all
dewma.before numeric tbc.sd - ewma.before
dewma.after numeric tbc.sd - ewma.after

Output Format

For the full cleangrowth() output schema (default columns, optional columns, and where the output trim happens), see vignette("wrapper") → Output Format.

The remainder of this section covers child-specific exclusion codes and CF rescue status.

Complete list of exclusion codes (child algorithm)

Output levels. The algorithm internally assigns the literal codes in the Code (internal) column below (these are what cleanchild() writes via .child_exc() and what every step’s logic, guards, and exclude.levels.peds use). At the end of cleangrowth(), a mapping layer translates each internal code into the two reported output levels:

  • Summary — the default exclude column. Consolidated across steps and across child/adult.
  • Detailed — the exclude_detailed column, added when exclude_detail = TRUE (or full_detail = TRUE). Step-level; keeps the -C- prefix; renames PIV→Hard-Limit. Shared same-day / preprocessing codes (Exclude-Identical, Exclude-Extraneous, Exclude-Missing-Info, Exclude-HC-Out-of-Range) carry no prefix at any level.

The internal literals are not renamed in the code; only the output is mapped. Canonical catalog + full crosswalk (incl. adult): vignette("exclusion-codes").

Code (internal) Child Step Param Detailed (exclude_detailed) Summary (exclude) Description
Include — All Include Include Value passes all checks
Exclude-Missing Init All Exclude-Missing-Info Exclude-Missing-Info Measurement is NA, NaN, < 0, == 0, or agedays < 0
Exclude-Not-Cleaned Init HEADCM Exclude-HC-Out-of-Range Exclude-HC-Out-of-Range HC with agedays > 5 × 365.25 (WHO HC z-score reference ends at 5 years)
Exclude-C-Temp-Same-Day 5 All — (internal only) — (internal only) Temp SDE; always resolved by Child Step 13 — if it survives to output, a warning is issued and the row maps to Exclude-Extraneous
Exclude-C-CF 6 All Exclude-C-CF Exclude-CF Value identical to prior-day value (not rescued)
Exclude-C-PIV 7 All Exclude-C-Hard-Limit Exclude-Hard-Limit Outside absolute or standardized population limits
Exclude-C-Evil-Twins 9 All Exclude-C-Evil-Twins Exclude-Extreme Adjacent extreme value pair/group
Exclude-C-Traj-Extreme 11 All Exclude-C-Traj-Extreme Exclude-Extreme Extreme EWMA outlier
Exclude-C-Identical 13 All Exclude-Identical Exclude-Identical Same-day duplicate with identical value
Exclude-C-Extraneous 13 All Exclude-Extraneous Exclude-Extraneous Same-day extraneous (resolved by EWMA, all extreme, or single day)
Exclude-C-Traj 15/16 All Exclude-C-Traj Exclude-Pattern Moderate EWMA outlier (all sub-rules collapsed)
Exclude-C-Abs-Diff 17 HT, HC Exclude-C-Abs-Diff Exclude-Pattern Height/HC velocity exceeded allowed limits
Exclude-C-Pair 19 All Exclude-C-Pair Exclude-Pair 2 measurements, one excluded
Exclude-C-Single 19 All Exclude-C-Single Exclude-Single Single measurement excluded
Exclude-C-Unevaluable-Trajectory 21 All Exclude-C-Unevaluable-Trajectory Exclude-Traj-Uneval Error ratio exceeds threshold

Note: Exclusion codes are not parameter-specific — the param is in the data row (e.g., Exclude-C-CF applies to WT, HT, and HC). The internal exclude.levels factor (exclude.levels.peds + exclude.levels.adult) enumerates the internal literals in the Code (internal) column plus the adult codes; the reported levels are enumerated separately in exclude.levels.summary / exclude.levels.detailed. A code outside the relevant level vector silently becomes NA when assigned to its factor, so any new code must be added to the matching vector before use.

CF rescue status (cf_detail = TRUE)

CF rescue tracking is opt-in via cf_detail = TRUE, which adds six columns to the output. The first three describe CF status/rescue; the last three (imperial, cf_dage_orig, cf_dage_next) are design aids for CF-logic development.

Column Values Meaning
cf_status "Orig" Originator of a CF string (value others were carried forward from)
"CF-R" CF candidate rescued (exclude set back to "Include")
"CF-NR" Carried-forward value not rescued (rescue-eligible but failed threshold; exclude stays "Exclude-C-CF")
"CF-E" Same-day-extraneous CF: a CF on a day that also has an Include. Never rescue-eligible; excluded from positional string detection (so no seq_win, no cf_deltaz); exclude stays "Exclude-C-CF".
"Not-CF" No CF relationship
"Adult" Adult row (CF detection not applicable)
cf_deltaz numeric \|sd.orig_uncorr - originator_z\| for positionally-detected CF candidates. NA for Orig, Not-CF, CF-E, and Adult rows.
cf_nextz numeric Signed change in sd.orig_uncorr from each CF row to the next non-CF Included value within (subjid, param), at the time of Step 6 (temp same-day-extraneous rows are skipped as both source and target). Populated for CF-R and CF-NR; NA for Orig, Not-CF, CF-E, Adult, and end-of-series cases.
imperial logical Row-level whole/half imperial-unit flag: TRUE when the value is a whole number of pounds (WEIGHTKG) or a whole/half inch (HEIGHTCM/HEADCM), within 0.01 of the imperial unit. Computed from v.orig, so populated for every row regardless of CF status; NA on missing-measurement rows (v.orig is NaN) and Adult rows.
cf_dage_orig integer Day gap from a CF row’s originator to the CF row (agedays_CF - agedays_orig; always positive). Populated for positionally-detected CF rows (CF-R/CF-NR); NA for Orig, Not-CF, CF-E, and Adult rows.
cf_dage_next numeric Day gap from a CF row (CF-R/CF-NR) to the next eligible non-CF Included value (agedays_next - agedays_CF; always positive) — the agedays analogue of cf_nextz, same eligibility rule. NA for Orig, Not-CF, CF-E, Adult, and end-of-series cases.

The internal cf_rescued column ("", "Rescued", "Rescued-All") is used during processing but is not returned in the default output.

Configurable Parameters

For wrapper-level parameters (id, adult_cutpoint, sd.recenter, ref.data.path, batch_size, parallel, num.batches, log.path, ref_tables, cached_results, changed_subjids, tri_exclude, quietly, adult_permissiveness, adult_scale_max_lbs), see vignette("wrapper") → Configurable Parameters (wrapper-level).

For the full cross-algorithm parameter and threshold index, see vignette("configuration").

This table covers child-specific algorithm parameters only. Per-step subsections under each Child Step section have more detailed descriptions.

Parameter Default Used in Description
cf_rescue "standard" Child Step 6 CF rescue mode: "standard" (deltaz/nextz rule), "none" (all CFs excluded), "all" (every detected CF rescued).
cf_detail FALSE Child Step 6 If TRUE, adds diagnostic columns cf_status, cf_deltaz, cf_nextz, imperial, cf_dage_orig, and cf_dage_next to the output.
piv.z.wt.high 22 Child Step 7 Upper CSD z cutoff for WEIGHTKG (all ages). Weight has no standardized low cutoff — low-WT z asymptotes near -10, so the absolute floors handle it.
piv.z.ht.low.young -25 Child Step 7 Lower CSD z cutoff for HEIGHTCM at ageyears < 1.
piv.z.ht.low.old -15 Child Step 7 Lower CSD z cutoff for HEIGHTCM at ageyears >= 1.
piv.z.ht.high 8 Child Step 7 Upper CSD z cutoff for HEIGHTCM (all ages).
piv.z.hc.low -15 Child Step 7 Lower CSD z cutoff for HEADCM (all ages).
piv.z.hc.high 15 Child Step 7 Upper CSD z cutoff for HEADCM (all ages).
ewma_window 15 Child Steps 11, 13, 15/16, 17 Maximum number of Include observations on each side that contribute to the EWMA weighting.
error.load.mincount 2 Child Step 21 Minimum count of exclusions before a subject-param can be evaluated for error-load escalation.
error.load.threshold 0.5 Child Step 21 Ratio of exclusions (numerator excludes SDE/CF/Missing/Not-Cleaned) above which all remaining Includes for the subject-param are excluded.

Complete List of Steps

Child Step Name Brief description
(preprocessing) Z-score calculation WHO/CDC CSD z-scores, blending
(preprocessing) Child Step 2b: GA correction Fenton-based correction for potcorr subjects
(preprocessing) Recentering Subtract population medians to create tbc.sd/ctbc.sd
Early 13 SDE-Identicals Remove same-day identical values before CF detection
5 Temporary SDE Temporarily flag same-day duplicates
6 Carried Forwards Identify and optionally rescue carried-forward values
7 PIV Exclude population implausible values (absolute + standardized)
9 Evil Twins Exclude adjacent extreme values
11 EWMA1 (Extreme) Exclude extreme EWMA outliers
13 Final SDE Resolve remaining same-day duplicates
15 EWMA2 (Moderate) Exclude moderate EWMA outliers
16 Birth HT/HC EWMA2 variant for birth height and HC values
17 Height/HC Velocity Exclude values exceeding velocity limits
19 Pairs and Singles Evaluate subjects with 1–2 remaining measurements
21 Error Load Exclude all if error ratio too high
22 Output Assemble return columns

Child Step numbers are not consecutive. Child Steps 3, 4, 8, 10, 12, 14, 18, and 20 either do not exist in the child algorithm or are handled within other steps.

Gestational Age Correction (Child Step 2b, preprocessing)

Child Step 2b runs in cleangrowth() preprocessing, before dispatch to cleanchild(). Full walkthrough — potcorr identification, Fenton merge, correction failure handling, the uncorr revert check, and checklist findings — lives in vignette("wrapper") → Gestational Age Correction (Child Step 2b, preprocessing).

In brief: subjects whose first weight is consistent with prematurity are flagged potcorr. Their z-scores are recomputed against the Fenton 2025 reference (using CSD method) at corrected age, with corrected WHO/CDC fallback above 2 years and a smoothed corrected→original blend over 2–4 years. The result is sd.corr, which downstream steps read through ctbc.sd.

Corroboration gate. A potcorr flag from a single low first weight is only trusted (correction kept) if true prematurity is corroborated by EITHER form 1 — a consistent early weight trajectory (the uncorr revert check: ≥2 weights < 2y whose corrected early spread is no worse than uncorrected), OR form 2 — a same-day height or head circumference whose own uncorrected z is <= -1.5 and no more than 3.5 z above the first weight’s uncorrected z (short, or small-headed, as well as light; the cap stops a merely-somewhat-low HT/HC from corroborating a far-lower weight). A subject corroborated by neither is reverted to uncorrected (uncorr = 1, sd.corr = sd.orig). This withdraws the false correction from a misplaced-decimal birth weight (low weight but a normal same-day length and no consistent early follow-up) so the implausible value is no longer hidden — it is then caught by the trajectory steps. Form 2 also keeps the correction for real short/small-headed preterms that the form-1 check alone over-reverted. Because form 1, height, and HC are ORed, HC only decides the outcome when there is no early-weight corroboration and no confirming height.

SDE Resolution (Child Step 13, including Early 13)

Scope All parameters
Exclusion codes Exclude-C-Identical, Exclude-C-Extraneous
Code location Early Child Step 13 is inline at the top of cleanchild() in child_clean.R. Main Child Step 13 is later in cleanchild() and reuses identify_temp_sde() (defined in child_clean.R).

Overview

Same-day extraneous (SDE) resolution runs in two phases:

  1. Early Child Step 13 (before Child Steps 5/6): Removes same-day identical values so they don’t inflate CF string detection
  2. Main Child Step 13 (after Child Step 11): Permanently resolves all remaining same-day duplicates using EWMA-based selection

SDE-Identicals (Early Child Step 13)

Prior step Preprocessing
Next step Child Step 5 (Temporary SDEs)

Runs on data.df before any other cleaning step.

Logic: 1. Sort by subjid, param, agedays, id 2. For each (subjid, param, agedays, v) group — same subject, parameter, day, AND measurement value — count Include rows 3. If count > 1, select one to keep via age-dependent id rule (birth: lowest id; other ages: highest id) 4. Others → Exclude-C-Identical

Handles partial identicals: [10.5, 10.5, 11.2] excludes one 10.5 but leaves 11.2 untouched.

Main Child Step 13

Prior step Child Step 11 (EWMA1)
Next step Child Step 15 (EWMA2)

After Child Steps 5–11 have removed PIVs, Evil Twins, extreme EWMA outliers, and carried forwards, Child Step 13 permanently resolves all remaining same-day duplicates. It works on a subset (data.sde) containing only subjects with same-day measurements, then merges results back.

Phase A: Setup

  1. Capture temp SDE ids for DOP median exclusion (temp_sde_ids_step13)
  2. Safety checks: warn if Include duplicates exist (these would indicate a bug in prior steps)
  3. Re-run identify_temp_sde() with exclude_from_dop_ids — in Child Step 13, temp SDEs are excluded from DOP median calculation (unlike Child Step 5 where all values contribute)
  4. Pre-filter to subjects with same-day measurements

Phase B1: SDE-Identicals (defensive)

A second SDE-Identicals pass runs on data.sde (Include + TempSDE rows). In the current implementation it is a no-op safety net: Early Child Step 13 already resolves all same-day same-value pairs on Include rows, v is never modified by any later step, and every temp-SDE rerun (Child Step 5 initial pass; Steps 6/7/9/11 post-step reruns; the Step 13 pre-phase identify_temp_sde() rerun, the one that passes exclude_from_dop_ids) preserves the one-Include-per-SPA invariant. cf_rescue = "all" restored values sit on different days than their CF originators, so they also cannot reintroduce same-day same-value pairs. The block is kept as a safety net against future invariant-breaking changes. Two checks, both age-dependent (birth: lowest internal_id; otherwise highest):

  1. Whole-day identical: All rows in the (subjid, param, agedays) group have the same v → keep one, others → Exclude-C-Identical. No exclude == "Include" guard — the check would demote a TempSDE loser to Identical if this block ever fired.
  2. Partial identical: Within a (subjid, param, agedays, v) group with more than one row, non-keepers → Exclude-C-Identical. Guarded on exclude == "Include".

Phase B2: One-Day SDE + SDE-All-Extreme

For subject-params with data on only ONE unique day (one_day_sde_flag):

  1. SDE-All-Extreme: If the closest value to the day’s median is still > 2 SD away, ALL values on that day are marked Exclude-C-Extraneous. Threshold: strict > 2.

  2. DOP medians: For one-day SDEs, cross-parameter medians provide a secondary sort key. DOP mapping: WT↔︎HT, HC→HT. Uses only fully included values (!was_temp_sde).

  3. One-Day selection: Sort by:

  • absdiff_rel_to_median ascending
  • absdiff_dop_for_sort ascending (Inf if NA)
  • Age-dependent internal_id tiebreaker Keep first; others → Exclude-C-Extraneous

Phase B3: SDE-EWMA resolution

For subject-params with data on multiple days:

  1. Compute EWMA from fully included non-temp-SDE values only. Robust drop: a lone far value (exclude == "Include", > 3 z from the SP provisional median, with n_far == 1 for the subject-param) is also excluded from this EWMA, mirroring the robust median drop in identify_temp_sde. This stops a lingering end-spike extreme — one the Evil Twins anchor rule cedes to Traj, so it is still Include at Step 13 — from inflating spa_ewma at an adjacent SDE day and falsely tripping SDE-All-Extreme into excluding both members of a plausible same-day pair.
  2. Assign EWMA values to temp SDEs by copying the ewma.all from their non-SDE same-day counterpart
  3. spa_ewma = max(ewma.all) per (subjid, param, agedays) — used as the reference point
  4. absdewma = |tbc.sd - spa_ewma| for each row
  5. SDE-All-Extreme: If min_absdewma > 1 for a group with 2+ eligible values, all → Exclude-C-Extraneous. Threshold: strict > 1.
  6. SDE-EWMA selection: Sort eligible values by absdewma ascending, age-dependent internal_id tiebreaker. Keep first → Include; others → Exclude-C-Extraneous

Phase B4: Merge back

SDE results are merged back to data.df by internal_id (data.df <- merge(data.df, sde_results, by = "internal_id", all.x = TRUE)). Only rows that were Include or Temp-SDE are overwritten — permanent exclusions from prior steps (PIV, Evil Twins, etc.) are preserved. Extra columns from SDE processing are dropped.

Rationale

  • EWMA-based tiebreaking for multi-day subject-params uses the trajectory context built up by the other cleaning steps. By the time Main Child Step 13 runs, extreme outliers (Child Steps 7, 9, 11) have been excluded, so the remaining EWMA is a reasonable proxy for the subject’s true trajectory.
  • One-day-only subject-params get a cross-parameter check (DOP) because without multiple days there is no trajectory to anchor selection; using the other parameter’s value (e.g., height for weight) adds a sanity check that the kept SDE is consistent with the child’s overall size.
  • SDE-All-Extreme thresholds (> 2 and > 1) are strict to avoid excluding entire SPAs when a single plausible value is within reach of the trajectory; the asymmetry between the one-day (> 2) and EWMA-based (> 1) thresholds reflects the weaker trajectory information available for one-day SPAs.
  • Two-phase SDE resolution (Early 13 → Child Step 5 → Main 13) separates the cheap, unambiguous case (same-day identicals) from the harder case (same-day non-identicals) so that CF string detection does not confuse identical SDEs with carried-forward strings.

Configurable parameters in scope for Child Step 13

None. The SDE-All-Extreme thresholds (> 2 one-day and > 1 EWMA-based) are hardcoded.

Variables created and dropped

All Main Child Step 13 working variables live on the data.sde subset (or on ewma_df, which is merged back into data.sde). They are not carried back to data.df at merge time: the Phase B4 block (the setdiff(names(data.df), keep_cols_sde) drop) drops every column not in keep_cols_sde (captured as keep_cols_sde <- names(data.df) before Phase B construction).

  • Phase B1 (defensive identicals): keep_id, keep_id_dup, dup_count — all dropped at the end of Phase B1.
  • Phase B2 (one-day): was_temp_sde, n_days_with_data, has_sde_day, one_day_sde_flag, median_tbc, absdiff_rel_to_median, min_absdiff_rel_to_median, HT_dop_med, WT_dop_med, HC_dop_med, absdiff_dop_med, absdiff_dop_for_sort, tiebreaker_oneday, keep_id_oneday.
  • Phase B3 (EWMA): ewma_df scratch columns (diff_before, diff_after, maxdiff, ageyears, exp_val, ewma.all, ewma.before, ewma.after), then on data.sde after the merge: ewma.all, ewma.before, ewma.after, spa_ewma, absdewma, n_available, min_absdewma, tiebreaker_ewma, keep_id_ewma. (ewma_fill is created and dropped inline.)

Inside the separately-called identify_temp_sde() (invoked from Phase A), the scratch columns median.spz, median.dopz, absdmedian.spz, absdmedian.dopz, extraneous.this.day, extraneous, and orig_row live on a copy of the input and are discarded when the helper returns — they never reach data.sde or data.df.

No persistent columns are added to data.df by Step 13.

Checklist findings

  1. DOP mapping consistent: All three Phase B2 DOP median branches (HT_dop_med, WT_dop_med, HC_dop_med) follow the canonical mapping — WT↔︎HT is symmetric, HC→HT is asymmetric (HC uses HT as its anchor; HT does not use HC).
  2. Boundaries: SDE-All-Extreme > 2 (one-day) and > 1 (EWMA-based) — both strict. Age-dependent id tiebreaker uses internal_id (not the user’s id) in Child Step 13.
  3. .child_valid() calls: Temp SDEs included when building the data.sde subset. EWMA computed on Include-only rows (excludes temp SDEs). DOP medians exclude temp SDEs via !was_temp_sde.
  4. Merge safety: Only Include and Temp-SDE rows are overwritten on merge-back — permanent exclusions from earlier steps are preserved.
  5. Parameter scope: All three params are handled. DOP has a param-specific mapping (see get_dop()).
  6. Factor levels: All SDE exclusion codes (Exclude-C-Identical, Exclude-C-Extraneous) exist in exclude.levels.

Temporary SDE Resolution (Child Step 5)

Scope All parameters
Operates on Valid rows (via .child_valid(), includes temp SDEs)
Prior step Early Child Step 13 (SDE-Identicals)
Next step Child Step 6 (Carried Forwards)
Exclusion code Exclude-C-Temp-Same-Day
Code location Inline in cleanchild() in child_clean.R; dispatches to identify_temp_sde() (defined in child_clean.R) without the Step-13 exclude_from_dop_ids argument.

Overview

After identical same-day values have been removed, remaining same-day duplicates (SDEs with different values) need temporary resolution. Child Step 5 selects the most plausible value for each same-day group and temporarily excludes the others. These temporary exclusions are revisited in Child Step 13 (final SDE resolution) after the dataset has been further cleaned.

The function identify_temp_sde() is also reused in Child Step 13 with different parameters (see Child Step 13).

Key terms

Term Meaning
SP median Subject-parameter median: median tbc.sd for a given subject and parameter, across all valid values
DOP median Designated-other-parameter median: the SP median of the other parameter for the same subject (WT↔︎HT, HC→HT)
absdmedian.spz Absolute distance from tbc.sd to SP median
absdmedian.dopz Absolute distance from tbc.sd to DOP median

Logic

Input: A data.table with columns id, internal_id, subjid, param, agedays, tbc.sd, exclude.

Child Step 5a — Identify SDE days: For each (subjid, param, agedays) group, if more than one valid row exists, all rows in the group are flagged as extraneous.this.day = TRUE.

Child Step 5b — Compute SP medians: For each (subjid, param), compute median.spz = median of tbc.sd across ALL valid values (not just non-SDE days). If exactly one Include value in the group sits > 3 z from the provisional median, it is dropped from this computation (robust SP median — see identify_temp_sde() in Support Functions); the same carve-out applies to the DOP median in the Step 13 path.

Child Step 5c — Compute DOP medians: For each parameter, look up the SP median of its designated other parameter for the same subject: - WEIGHTKG → uses HEIGHTCM median - HEIGHTCM → uses WEIGHTKG median - HEADCM → uses HEIGHTCM median

In Child Step 5, all valid values contribute to DOP medians. In Child Step 13 (when this function is reused), temp SDEs are excluded from DOP median calculation via the exclude_from_dop_ids parameter.

Child Step 5d — Compute distances: For each SDE-day row: - absdmedian.spz = |tbc.sd - median.spz| - absdmedian.dopz = |tbc.sd - median.dopz|

Fallback logic when medians are unavailable: 1. If SP median is NA but DOP median exists: use DOP median as the primary distance (absdmedian.spz = |tbc.sd - median.dopz|) 2. If both medians are NA: use |tbc.sd| (treat 0 as median) 3. If DOP median is NA: set absdmedian.dopz = Inf (sorts last)

Child Step 5e — Select value to keep: Within each (subjid, param, agedays) group, sort by: 1. absdmedian.spz ascending (closest to SP median first) 2. absdmedian.dopz ascending (closest to DOP median as tiebreaker) 3. Age-dependent internal_id tiebreaker: - At birth (agedays == 0): internal_id ascending (keep lowest) - At other ages: internal_id descending (keep highest)

The first value after sorting is kept; all others are marked extraneous = TRUE.

Child Step 5f — Return result: The function returns a logical vector in the original row order (not the keyby-sorted order), indicating which rows should be excluded. The caller sets these rows to Exclude-C-Temp-Same-Day.

Rationale

  • SP median as primary: The value closest to the subject’s own trajectory (as captured by their median z-score) is most likely correct.
  • DOP median as tiebreaker: When two values are equally close to the SP median, the one whose z-score is also closer to the other parameter’s median is preferred. This cross-parameter check adds robustness.
  • Temporary resolution: This is intentionally conservative — the dataset still contains errors that could distort medians. Child Step 13 re-resolves SDEs after PIV, Evil Twins, and EWMA1 have removed more extreme values.

Safety check

After Child Step 5, the code verifies that no (subjid, param, agedays) group has more than one Include row. If duplicates are found, a warning is issued (indicates a bug in identify_temp_sde()).

Configurable parameters in scope for Child Step 5

None. Selection uses SP median → DOP median → age-dependent internal_id tiebreaker — all hardcoded.

Variables created and dropped

  • v.orig (created): A copy of the measurement values before any transformations. Created just before Child Step 5 runs, used in Child Step 6 CF detection (exact value comparison) and the imperial design-aid flag. Dropped after Child Step 6.
  • subj.dup (created): List of subject IDs that have at least one temporary SDE. Used for efficiency in later steps that only need to process SDE subjects.

Checklist findings

  1. DOP mapping consistent: WT↔︎HT, HC→HT, same mapping used in Child Step 13.
  2. Boundaries: Ties broken by age-dependent internal_id (lowest at birth, highest otherwise).
  3. .child_valid() call: Includes temp SDEs (the candidates being resolved).
  4. Parameter scope: All three params handled.
  5. Factor level: Exclude-C-Temp-Same-Day exists in exclude.levels.
  6. Safety check: Post-step verifier has never fired in testing.

Carried Forwards (Child Step 6)

Scope All parameters
Prior step Child Step 5 (Temporary SDEs)
Next step Child Step 7 (PIV)
Exclusion code Exclude-C-CF
Controlled by cf_rescue ("standard" default / "none" / "all"); cf_detail for diagnostic columns
Code location CF logic inline in cleanchild() in R/child_clean.R

A carried-forward (CF) value is a measurement exactly equal to the prior day’s value for the same subject and parameter — typically a data-entry artifact rather than an independent re-measurement. Child Step 6 detects CFs, groups them into strings (a non-CF originator followed by the CFs that copy it forward), and either leaves them Exclude-C-CF or rescues them back to Include. In the default cf_rescue = "standard" mode, each string is decided on its last CF using the deltaz/nextz rule: rescue when the carried value lies on a plausibly flat or declining trajectory, exclude when it is a notch the child has grown past. cf_rescue = "none" excludes all detected CFs; cf_rescue = "all" rescues all of them. Optional cf_detail = TRUE adds six diagnostic columns (cf_status, cf_deltaz, cf_nextz, and three development aids).

This step is documented in full — detection, the five processing phases, the rescue rule and its rationale, and the diagnostic columns — in vignette("cf-rescue").

PIV: Population Implausible Values (Child Step 7)

Scope All parameters
Operates on Valid rows including temp SDEs
Prior step Child Step 6 (Carried Forwards)
Next step Child Step 9 (Evil Twins)
Exclusion codes Exclude-C-PIV
Code location Inline in cleanchild() in R/child_clean.R

Overview

PIV identifies measurements that are biologically impossible or extremely unlikely, using two blocks applied in sequence:

  1. Absolute PIV — fixed limits on raw measurements (v).
  2. Standardized PIV — cutoffs on unrecentered CSD z-scores (sd.orig_uncorr).

After both blocks, the temp-SDE identification is rerun: all rows currently flagged Exclude-C-Temp-Same-Day are reset to Include, then identify_temp_sde() is called against the post-PIV state.

The working valid_set is computed once at the top of the step via .child_valid(data.df, include.temporary.extraneous = TRUE) and reused across both PIV blocks. It contains Include rows and rows flagged Exclude-C-Temp-Same-Day; any other non-Include row is not in valid_set and so cannot be assigned Exclude-C-PIV here.

Absolute PIV thresholds

All conditions use strict < or > (not <= / >=). Rows that match get exclude := "Exclude-C-PIV".

Weight (WEIGHTKG):

Condition Threshold Rationale
First year (agedays <= 365) v < 0.2 kg Published minimum viable birth weight; allows preterm
After first year (agedays > 365) v < 1 kg Minimum plausible outpatient weight
Birth (agedays == 0) v > 10.5 kg Maximum plausible birth weight
ageyears < 2 v > 35 kg Highest plausible weight under 2y
Any age v > 600 kg Published maximum human weight

Height (HEIGHTCM):

Condition Threshold Rationale
Any age v < 18 cm Fenton z=-6 at 22 0/7 weeks
Any age v > 244 cm Published maximum
Birth (agedays == 0) v > 65 cm Fenton z=6 at 40 0/7 weeks

Head circumference (HEADCM):

Condition Threshold Rationale
Any age v < 13 cm Fenton z=-6 at 22 0/7 weeks
Any age v > 75 cm Published maximum
Birth (agedays == 0) v > 50 cm Birth maximum

Standardized PIV thresholds

Uses unrecentered z-scores (sd.orig_uncorr). Each cutoff is a user-settable cleangrowth() parameter; the default values (shown in the tables below) preserve the prior hardcoded behavior. The standardized block adds a !grepl(piv_pattern, exclude) guard where piv_pattern <- "^Exclude-C-PIV$". The guard skips rows that the absolute block just assigned Exclude-C-PIV, because valid_set was computed before the absolute block ran and therefore still treats those rows as valid. Rows originally flagged Exclude-C-Temp-Same-Day that were not hit by absolute PIV can be overwritten to Exclude-C-PIV by the standardized block; the 7d temp-SDE rerun afterward re-flags any SPA that still has a duplicate Include.

Weight:

Condition Threshold Parameter (default)
Any age sd.orig_uncorr > piv.z.wt.high piv.z.wt.high (22)

Weight has no standardized low cutoff. With a positive weight the CSD z below the median asymptotes to -M/csd_neg as the weight → 0, peaking near -10 in infancy and shallower elsewhere, so a low-weight z can never reach a useful cutoff — the absolute floors (v < 0.2 kg under 1y, v < 1 kg after) catch low weights instead. (HT/HC keep their low cutoffs because those z’s are reachable just above their absolute floors.)

Height:

Condition Threshold Parameter (default)
ageyears < 1 sd.orig_uncorr < piv.z.ht.low.young piv.z.ht.low.young (-25)
ageyears >= 1 sd.orig_uncorr < piv.z.ht.low.old piv.z.ht.low.old (-15)
Any age sd.orig_uncorr > piv.z.ht.high piv.z.ht.high (8)

The default upper HT cutoff (piv.z.ht.high = 8) is tighter than the default upper WT cutoff (piv.z.wt.high = 22). Analysis of CHOP data showed the ±15/±25 range was too loose for heights.

Head circumference:

Condition Threshold Parameter (default)
Any age sd.orig_uncorr < piv.z.hc.low piv.z.hc.low (-15)
Any age sd.orig_uncorr > piv.z.hc.high piv.z.hc.high (15)

Temp SDE re-evaluation (7d)

After both PIV blocks, all rows currently flagged Exclude-C-Temp-Same-Day are reset to Include, then identify_temp_sde() is rerun on the full dataset. This mirrors the Step-5 temp-SDE logic against the post-PIV state: if the prior temp-SDE keeper on an SPA has just been excluded as PIV, another value in that SPA becomes the flagged duplicate.

Rationale

  • Two PIV blocks (absolute then standardized) catch complementary error modes. Absolute PIV catches data that is impossible regardless of reference standard (e.g., a weight of 800 kg). Standardized PIV catches values that are plausible in isolation but implausible for a human (e.g., a height z-score of -50 — the raw value might be 30 cm, which is within the absolute range but impossibly short).
  • Different age bands for the lower HT standardized cutoff (< 1 year vs. >= 1 year): Very young children, especially preterm infants, have much wider normal z-score ranges on the low side; CDC/WHO references don’t extend to very low birth weights, so an uncorrected z-score of -20 can be normal for a 24-week preemie whose Fenton-corrected z-score is within range. (Weight has no lower standardized cutoff — see the Weight note above.)
  • Tighter upper HT cutoff (piv.z.ht.high = 8) than upper WT (piv.z.wt.high = 22): Heights are bounded biologically; a z-score of 22 for height is impossible, while extremely high weight z-scores are observed in real severe-obesity data.
  • Uncorrected z-scores (sd.orig_uncorr): Standardized PIV runs against the reference z-score (pre-GA-correction) because the PIV cutoffs are meant to catch data-entry errors — applying GA correction first would narrow the range on a preterm weight and make the PIV boundary effectively parameter-specific.
  • Temp SDE re-evaluation after PIV: PIV can exclude the row that was the temp-SDE keeper on an SPA, at which point a different value in the SPA needs to be promoted to Include and the other values re-flagged.

Configurable parameters in scope for Child Step 7

Six user-settable standardized-PIV cutoffs: piv.z.wt.high (22), piv.z.ht.low.young (-25), piv.z.ht.low.old (-15), piv.z.ht.high (8), piv.z.hc.low (-15), piv.z.hc.high (15). Weight has no standardized low cutoff. Absolute-PIV thresholds (v < 0.2 kg, v > 244 cm, etc.) are not user-configurable.

Variables created and dropped

  • ageyears: created at the top of Child Step 7 for age- threshold checks, dropped at the end of the step.
  • piv_pattern: local character scalar ("^Exclude-C-PIV$") used only as the standardized-block guard; not stored on data.df.

Checklist findings

  1. Operates-on set. .child_valid(data.df, include.temporary.extraneous = TRUE) is the correct selector for PIV: Include plus temp-SDE rows.
  2. Boundaries — all strict inequalities. < 0.2, < 1, > 10.5, > 244, < -25, > 22, etc. A weight of exactly 0.2 kg is not excluded; a standardized z of exactly -25 is not excluded.
  3. Age boundary at 1 year. Standardized PIV uses ageyears < 1 vs. ageyears >= 1. A child whose ageyears equals 1 exactly falls in the >= 1 band.
  4. Valid-set refresh. valid_set is computed once and not refreshed between absolute and standardized PIV. The standardized block relies on the !grepl(piv_pattern, exclude) guard to skip rows just assigned Exclude-C-PIV by the absolute block. Non- Include rows other than Exclude-C-Temp-Same-Day are not in valid_set and so cannot be overwritten by Child Step 7.
  5. Parameter scope. All three params (WEIGHTKG, HEIGHTCM, HEADCM) have their own absolute and standardized thresholds.
  6. Factor levels. Exclude-C-PIV is present in exclude.levels; absolute and standardized PIV share the single code.
  7. Standardized-PIV cutoffs are parameterized. Eight piv.z.* cutoffs flow from cleangrowth() through cleanchild() into Child Step 7. See the “Configurable parameters in scope for Child Step 7” subsection above.

Evil Twins (Child Step 9)

Scope All parameters
Operates on Valid rows, excluding temp SDEs, requiring ≥ 4 eligible (Include, non-temp-SDE) values per subject-param
Prior step Child Step 7 (PIV)
Next step Child Step 11 (EWMA1 — Extreme EWMA)
Exclusion code Exclude-C-Evil-Twins
Code location Inline in cleanchild() in child_clean.R; support function calc_otl_evil_twins() also defined in child_clean.R

Overview

Evil Twins identifies adjacent measurements that are both extreme — pairs (or longer runs) of consecutive values far from the subject’s trajectory. When two such values sit next to each other, each distorts the other’s EWMA enough that neither looks out of line on a neighbor-weighted basis, so EWMA-based exclusions alone can miss them. Child Step 9 catches these values before EWMA processing (Child Steps 11, 15, 16).

The step is iterative: it finds the single worst OTL value, excludes it, recalculates OTL on the remaining values, and repeats until no OTL values remain.

OTL calculation (calc_otl_evil_twins)

A value is “over the limit” (OTL) only as an inner member of an anchored over-the-limit pair. Within a subject-param (rows in agedays order, internal_id breaking same-day ties), consider four consecutive eligible values A, B, C, D. The inner pair (B, C) is over the limit when its jump exceeds 5 on both tbc.sd and ctbc.sd; the pair is anchored when each outer flank holds its adjacent inner member to within 3 on either scale (A anchors B, D anchors C). When both hold, B and C are flagged OTL; the outer anchors A and D are not.

Key details: - Jump threshold is strict > 5 on both scales (a difference of exactly 5.0 is not over the limit); anchor threshold is <= 3 on either scale. Requiring both tbc.sd and ctbc.sd to jump prevents false positives where gestational-age correction moves one z-score across the boundary for potcorr subjects but the other does not. - A missing flank (no A before B, or no D after C) disqualifies the pair, so single end-spikes and singles embedded between normal values are ceded to the EWMA steps rather than excluded here. - “Flank”/“adjacent” means the next or prior rows within the same (subjid, param); shift() with by = .(subjid, param) returns NA at subject-param boundaries, so comparisons never cross subject-params (relies on the caller’s sort). - A subject-param with fewer than 4 rows cannot form an A,B,C,D group, so otl is FALSE for all of its rows.

Child Step 9 main logic

9a. Setup: - Sort data.df by (subjid, param, agedays, internal_id) — this must happen before valid_set is computed, because valid_set is a boolean vector aligned to row position; reordering after would misalign it. - Require ≥ 4 eligible (Include, non-temp-SDE) values per (subjid, param) — the anchor rule needs an A,B,C,D group, so an SP with fewer than 4 eligible values cannot form an evaluable pair. (sp_count_9 is now the count of eligible rows, not all rows.) - valid_set: .child_valid(data.df, include.temporary.extraneous = FALSE) combined with sp_count_9 >= 4L. Temp SDEs do not participate. - OTL detection is the 4-value anchor rule (calc_otl_evil_twins()): a value is OTL only as an inner member (B or C) of an over-the-limit pair whose jump exceeds 5 on BOTH tbc.sd and ctbc.sd, flanked by stable outer values (A before B, D after C) each within 3 on EITHER scale. Single end-spikes and singles embedded between normals are ceded to EWMA. - Run calc_otl_evil_twins() once on the initial valid rows as a cheap early exit — if no row is OTL globally, skip the per-group loop entirely.

9b. Per-group processing: If any row in the initial pass is OTL, iterate over the unique (subjid, param) groups that contain at least one OTL row. For each group:

  1. Extract the valid rows for that (subjid, param) as a local df copy (group sizes are typically 3–30 rows).
  2. Skip groups with fewer than 2 valid rows.
  3. While loop — iterate until no OTL remains (or fewer than 4 eligible Include values remain, ceding the rest to the EWMA steps). Each pass excludes ONE OTL value, selected by this precedence:
    • a. UER3. If the group’s values split cleanly into a unit-error group (the per-value unit_error_range flag) and a plausible group (|tbc.sd| < 3), the unit-error group is all one z-sign, and the raw-measurement mean ratio of the two groups is in the unit-conversion band (WT 1.7–2.7, HT/HC 2.04–3.04), exclude the OTL candidate with the highest |tbc.sd| (the unit-error block).
    • b. Windowed-median band. Otherwise, for each OTL candidate compute med_diff = |tbc.sd − windowed_median|, where the windowed median is the median of Include tbc.sd within ±4 positions of the candidate (kept local so a long declining/varied tail elsewhere can’t collapse the gap between a lone dip and an on-trajectory plateau). If ≥2 candidates fall within 1 of the maximum med_diff (a bunch the median can’t discriminate), exclude the highest |tbc.sd| among them, tiebreak internal_id.
    • c. Otherwise exclude the highest med_diff, tiebreak |tbc.sd|, tiebreak internal_id. Mark the selected row Exclude-C-Evil-Twins, recompute OTL on the remaining Include rows, and continue. (unit_error_range per-value flags are computed once per batch at Step 9 entry.)
  4. Collect line values of all rows marked Exclude-C-Evil-Twins in this group.

9c. Apply exclusions: After all groups are processed, the accumulated et_excl_lines are applied to data.df in a single bulk update setting exclude := .child_exc(param, "Evil-Twins") (which produces "Exclude-C-Evil-Twins" — .child_exc() accepts but ignores the param argument; codes are not param-specific).

9d. Cleanup and temp-SDE re-evaluation: - Drop sp_count_9. - Reset every row currently flagged Exclude-C-Temp-Same-Day to Include, then rerun identify_temp_sde() against the post-Evil-Twins state. Only the temp-SDE identification is rerun, not all of Child Step 5.

Rationale

  • “Adjacent extreme pair” problem: Two consecutive implausible values separated by |Δz| > 5 from each other — but each within 5 of the next or prior value — would each have a plausible-looking EWMA dominated by the other twin. EWMA-based steps (11, 15, 16) would miss both. Child Step 9 catches these before EWMA runs.
  • Dual requirement (both tbc.sd AND ctbc.sd must differ by > 5): For potcorr subjects, Fenton correction can shift one z-score (ctbc.sd) while leaving the other (tbc.sd) near a boundary, or vice versa. Requiring both to trigger prevents false positives where one correction pipeline sees a jump but the other does not.
  • Iterative with one exclusion per pass: Excluding the single worst value and recomputing may break the OTL condition for the other value (it was only OTL because of its twin). This avoids excluding legitimate measurements as collateral damage.
  • 4+ eligible values required: The anchor rule needs an A,B,C,D group, so a subject-param with fewer than 4 eligible values cannot form an evaluable pair; Pairs/Singles (Child Step 19) handles short subject-params separately.
  • internal_id tiebreaker (not age-dependent): By the time Child Step 9 runs, PIV and CF have already resolved the types of birth-specific cases that need age-dependent handling; using a simple deterministic tiebreaker here is sufficient.

Configurable parameters in scope for Child Step 9

None. The OTL jump threshold (> 5 on both tbc.sd and ctbc.sd) and the anchor threshold (<= 3 on either scale) are hardcoded in calc_otl_evil_twins().

Variables created and dropped

  • elig_9 (created): per-row eligibility flag (.child_valid(..., include.temporary.extraneous = FALSE)). Dropped at the end of Child Step 9.
  • sp_count_9 (created): per-(subjid, param) count of eligible rows, used as the >= 4L gate. Dropped at the end of Child Step 9.
  • otl (local to calc_otl_evil_twins()): OTL flag per row, recomputed each iteration within a group; not persisted to data.df.

Checklist findings

  1. Count gate is a correctness gate, not an optimization: sp_count_9 counts eligible (Include, non-temp-SDE) rows, and the >= 4L gate is required — deleting it would index past the ends of a short subject-param. Groups that pass are re-checked at length(grp_idx) < 4L, and nrow(incl) < 4L is re-checked at the top of each iteration.
  2. Boundaries: OTL threshold is strict > 5. Tiebreaker uses lowest internal_id (not age-dependent, unlike the birth-aware tiebreaking used by SDE steps).
  3. .child_valid() flags: include.temporary.extraneous = FALSE — Evil Twins operates on the “clean” view without SDE candidates.
  4. Sort order: Explicit order(subjid, param, agedays, internal_id) before valid_set computation. The inline comment explains why the sort must precede valid_set: reordering after would misalign the boolean vector against row positions.
  5. Both tbc.sd and ctbc.sd used: The dual threshold prevents false positives for potcorr subjects, where gestational-age correction can move one z-score close to the neighbor but not the other.
  6. Parameter scope: All three params (WEIGHTKG, HEIGHTCM, HEADCM) are handled uniformly; Evil Twins is not param-specific.
  7. Factor levels: Exclude-C-Evil-Twins is present in exclude.levels.
  8. Efficiency: Cheap early exit via a single global calc_otl_evil_twins() call; only subject-params with OTL rows enter the per-group loop; groups are small and independent. The while loop typically runs 1–3 iterations per group.

EWMA1: Extreme EWMA (Child Step 11)

Scope All parameters
Operates on Include values only (no temp SDEs), requiring 3+ per subject-param
Prior step Child Step 9 (Evil Twins)
Next step Child Step 13 (Final SDE Resolution)
Exclusion code Exclude-C-Traj-Extreme
Code location Inline in cleanchild() in child_clean.R; support functions ewma() and identify_temp_sde() also defined in child_clean.R

Overview

EWMA1 flags extreme outliers using Exponentially Weighted Moving Averages. For each measurement, the EWMA predicts what the value “should” be based on the subject’s other measurements for the same parameter. Values that deviate extremely from their EWMA prediction are flagged Exclude-C-Traj-Extreme.

The step is iterative at two levels: 1. Per subject-param: flag at most ONE worst value per pass. 2. Globally: repeat across all subject-params until no new exclusions occur.

This prevents one extreme value from distorting the EWMA of nearby values and causing a cascade of false exclusions.

The step has three blocks:

  • 11a. Pre-filter / setup — count and extreme filters narrow the set of subject-params, and the subjects that currently carry temp SDEs are captured for later targeted recalculation.
  • 11b. Iteration loop — global while loop; each pass processes one subject-param at a time via a per-group closure, flags at most one worst value, and runs a targeted temp-SDE recalc.
  • 11c. End-of-step temp SDE refresh — global reset + rerun of identify_temp_sde() before Child Step 13.

11a. Pre-filter / setup

Two pre-filters narrow the work:

  1. Count filter: Subject-params with <= 2 Include values are skipped (EWMA needs 3+ points).
